verses.text: No sooner is the Israel-Judah quarrel begun than Sheba, a worthless Benjamite, blows a trumpet: 'We have no part in David,' and the men of Israel desert their king. A spark of pride finds dry tinder. Where hearts are already divided, one rabble-rouser can scatter a kingdom.

No sooner is the Israel-Judah quarrel begun than Sheba, a worthless Benjamite, blows a trumpet: 'We have no part in David,' and the men of Israel desert their king. A spark of pride finds dry tinder. Where hearts are already divided, one rabble-rouser can scatter a kingdom.

David shuts away the ten concubines Absalom had defiled, providing for them but living with them no more, widowed all their days. The innocent still bear the long shadow of others' sin. David cares for them, yet the wound his own sin opened cannot simply be closed.

David orders Amasa to raise Judah within three days, but Amasa delays, so David sends Abishai and the standing troops after Sheba before the revolt can grow. Swift danger calls for swift action; a leader cannot let a hesitant man's delay give the enemy time to gather.

David orders Amasa to raise Judah within three days, but Amasa delays, so David sends Abishai and the standing troops after Sheba before the revolt can grow. Swift danger calls for swift action; a leader cannot let a hesitant man's delay give the enemy time to gather.

Joab, jealous of Amasa's command, greets him with a kiss and stabs him dead, then marches on as if nothing happened. The smiling embrace hides a dagger. There is no treachery more vile than murder done under the cover of friendship and a kiss.

Joab, jealous of Amasa's command, greets him with a kiss and stabs him dead, then marches on as if nothing happened. The smiling embrace hides a dagger. There is no treachery more vile than murder done under the cover of friendship and a kiss.

Joab, jealous of Amasa's command, greets him with a kiss and stabs him dead, then marches on as if nothing happened. The smiling embrace hides a dagger. There is no treachery more vile than murder done under the cover of friendship and a kiss.

The army halts, transfixed by Amasa wallowing in his blood in the road, until he is dragged aside and covered. Then they follow Joab again. Bloodshed stops a people in their tracks; yet how quickly the crowd moves on, leaving the murderer still in command.

The army halts, transfixed by Amasa wallowing in his blood in the road, until he is dragged aside and covered. Then they follow Joab again. Bloodshed stops a people in their tracks; yet how quickly the crowd moves on, leaving the murderer still in command.

Sheba shuts himself in the city of Abel, and Joab's army throws up a mound and begins to batter the wall. The revolt has shrunk to one man behind one gate. Rebellion against God's order, however loud at first, narrows at last to a corner with no escape.

Sheba shuts himself in the city of Abel, and Joab's army throws up a mound and begins to batter the wall. The revolt has shrunk to one man behind one gate. Rebellion against God's order, however loud at first, narrows at last to a corner with no escape.

A wise woman of Abel calls over the wall, pleading that Joab not destroy 'a mother in Israel,' a peaceable and faithful city, for one rebel's sake. One voice of sense can speak where armies only batter. Wisdom seeks to save the many rather than punish all for the few.

A wise woman of Abel calls over the wall, pleading that Joab not destroy 'a mother in Israel,' a peaceable and faithful city, for one rebel's sake. One voice of sense can speak where armies only batter. Wisdom seeks to save the many rather than punish all for the few.

Joab asks only for Sheba; the woman has the rebel's head thrown over the wall, and the siege lifts. Her counsel spares the city. The surrender of one guilty man saves a multitude — a small picture of how the giving up of one life can be the saving of many.

The chapter ends with a roll of David's officers — Joab over the army, Benaiah over the guard, others over the labour and the records. Order is restored; the machinery of the kingdom resumes. After every upheaval, God brings His people back to steady, ordered service.
